The apparent rarity of this species in their habitat and high levels of\naggression between consexuals suggests the possibility of some sort of plasticity in their primary sex\ndetermination. In this study, the inﬂuence of social environment upon primary sex determination in\nH. picta was examined experimentally in the laboratory. Naı¨ve juveniles were placed in three novel social\nenvironments: paired juveniles, single juveniles, and adult/juvenile pairs. Spacing behavior and the onset\nof external differentiation were observed during long-term experiments for each social treatment. Gonadal\ndevelopment was also observed. Spacing behavior of paired shrimp reﬂected the sexual composition of\nsocial groups; high intrapair distance (IPD) for same-sex pairs and low IPD for opposite-sex pairs. Sex\ndetermination results were not so clear. Two of the three paired juvenile replicates displayed phenotype\nfrequencies different from those expected from a random sample of gonochoristic organisms with a 1:1\nsex ratio. However, the third replicate and the combined frequencies did not show any statistical difference\nfrom the hypothetical random sample. Furthermore, single juveniles and those paired with adults\nexpressed sex in nearly 1:1 ratios, regardless of the sex of adult conspeciﬁcs. Histological examination of\njuvenile shrimp conﬁrmed that sex was determined as early as four weeks after larval metamorphosis.\nTherefore, social environment had no clear effect upon sexual phenotype expressed. However, single\nfemales attained puberty at a signiﬁcantly greater age and larger size than did females paired with males.\nHence for H. picta, social environment had a measurable effect on the timing of female puberty. This is the\nﬁrst demonstration of this phenomenon in decapod Crustacea.\n\nThe phenomenon of environmental sex de-\ntermination (ESD) (Bull, 1983) is well docu-\nmented in many teleost ﬁsh families in the\nform of some sort of sequential hermaphrodit-\nism (Atz, 1964; Smith, 1975; Yogo, 1987).\nThe best such examples in ﬁsh involve the\nsocial environment and its effect on sex (e.g.,\nFricke and Fricke, 1977; Moyer and Nakazono,\n1978; Warner, 1982). In crustaceans, however,\nthere appears to be little direct evidence of so-\ncial control of functional sex. The best known\nexamples occur in parasitic bopyrid isopods\nand protandrous hermaphrodite shrimp of the\nthe\nfamily Pandalidae. In bopyrid examples,\nﬁrst individual to reach a host becomes female\nand may cause subsequent conspeciﬁcs to be-\ncome male (Reverberi, 1944; Reinhard, 1949;\nCharniaux-Cotton et al., 1983).\nIn pandalid\nshrimp, the timing of male-female sex change\nis thought\nto be inﬂuenced by the demo-\ngraphics of their populations (Charnov, 1982).\nHowever, the mechanism of such a system is\nin doubt (Bergstro¨m, 1997).\n\napproximately\n\nExamples of hermaphroditism in the mala-\ncostracan Crustacea are relatively uncommon\nand usually are in the form of protandrous sex\nchange.
